symbols: draped white curtains — purity and the bridal chamber of the Song of Solomon
marble interior — the splendor of Solomon's palace and the beauty of holiness
central reclining group of women — the daughters of Jerusalem who attend the beloved
warm and cool drapery colors — the interplay of passion and purity in the Song's love poetry
contemplative postures — the interior life of love and spiritual longing
description: A frieze-like composition set in a classical marble interior with latticed windows. A group of young women in flowing Greco-Roman drapery are arranged across the scene. At center, several women recline and sit together in languid, contemplative poses on a draped platform. To the left, a standing figure holds back a white curtain, while at the right another figure stands beside a potted plant. The palette is dominated by whites, creams, and warm orange-red tones, giving the scene a dreamy, classical atmosphere.
theological_description: Albert Joseph Moore's painting, though rendered in the Aesthetic Movement's classical idiom, takes its title from the Song of Solomon's beloved — the Shulamite (Song 6:13). The scene evokes the daughters of Jerusalem gathered with the bride, a recurring motif in the Song's dramatic poetry. In Jewish tradition the Song was read as an allegory of God's love for Israel; Christian exegesis from Origen onward interpreted the Shulamite as a figure of the Church, the soul, or the Virgin Mary, and her companions as the faithful community awaiting the Bridegroom. The marble setting recalls Solomon's palatial splendor described in 1 Kings, while the languid postures suggest the longing expressed in Song 3:1 — 'on my bed by night I sought him whom my soul loves.' Moore's aestheticized treatment strips the narrative specificity but preserves the contemplative atmosphere of desire, beauty, and anticipation that is the Song's theological heart.

scripture_refs.reason: The Shulamite is directly named — 'Return, return, O Shulamite' — as the title figure of the painting
The Shulamite describing herself to the daughters of Jerusalem, who surround her in this scene
The beloved's longing and searching for her lover, reflected in the contemplative and yearning postures
The charge to the daughters of Jerusalem not to stir up love until it pleases — the restful, waiting quality of the scene
